item.title: Less than 20km road yet to be gravelled: Holemba
item.description: LESS than 20 kilometres of the new 168km transnational highway is yet to be gravelled so that members of the public can use it, says Works and Highways Secretary Gibson Holemba. “We have done gravel works for the entire 168km road and we are already closing in, with less than 20km to go,” Holemba said.
item.content:encoded:
LESS than 20 kilometres of the new 168km transnational highway is yet to be gravelled so that members of the public can use it, says Works and Highways Secretary Gibson Holemba.

“We have done gravel works for the entire 168km road and we are already closing in, with less than 20km to go,” Holemba said.

The road which was completed last weekend will allow people to travel by road from Port Moresby to Lae.

Right now, he said that the entire 168-kilometre corridor had been opened through to basic road formation standard by the Department’s plant and transport services division.

Building a road to a basic road formation standard means that the construction of the road corridor has been completed and shaped for vehicle access.

It involves clearing trees and vegetation, cutting through hills or levelling terrain, forming the road alignment and width, building an earth or gravel surface, basic drainage works like culverts or side drains, making the route passable for vehicles and machinery,

The next stage will be gravelling the road, sealing/bitumen/asphalt, building permanent bridges, putting up proper shoulders and safety barriers, drainage and slope protection and high-quality finishing work.

The priority now is to undertake selective gravelling on critical sections, particularly steep gradients, soft ground areas, high rainfall zones, river crossing approaches, and sections with weak subgrade conditions to ensure all-weather accessibility and operational stability.

Holemba said the tar-sealing phase would be done in phases, subject to detailed engineering design, traffic growth, geotechnical assessments, and the availability of funding from the Government and development partners.

“The immediate priority of government under Connect PNG is to establish and maintain basic connectivity first, followed by upgrading to gravel standard and eventually sealed pavement standard over time,” he said.

 Holemba said that the K60 million funding allocated so far was for the corridor opening, earthworks, drainage structures, formation cutting, bridging works, plant mobilisation, logistics, and basic access construction over the last four years.

The funding does not cover the gravelling and sealing components for the entire corridor, he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Fifth wave of fish deaths hits Madina
item.description: MORE fish and other marine life have been washed up on the shores of Madina in New Ireland – the fifth wave of fish deaths since December last year. Reports by Ailan Awareness, which has been closely monitoring the situation, show that fish deaths slowed after the fourth wave about three weeks ago. But it began increasing again this week.
item.content:encoded:
MORE fish and other marine life have been washed up on the shores of Madina in New Ireland – the fifth wave of fish deaths since December last year.

Reports by Ailan Awareness, which has been closely monitoring the situation, show that fish deaths slowed after the fourth wave about three weeks ago. But it began increasing again this week.

Fish started dying at Madina, Panafau and Luapul.

Ailan Awareness founder John Aini confirmed that most of the findings were concentrated in Madina, with the possibility of more unaccounted for.

“The number in Madina is much too high,” he said.

“There is definitely a lot more than the number we are putting out publicly if fish are dying in areas we cannot see.”

Dogs have also reportedly died after eating the dead fish.

Meanwhile, the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) has released water and fish tissue sample results to the Provincial Disaster Office.

But the findings have not been made public.

A separate report by University of Papua New Guinea scientists who visited the affected areas in March suggests that the most likely source of pollution is linked to oil palm activities, including fertilisers, pesticides and palm oil mill waste entering the sea through runoff.

The team also observed dying sardines, fish with popped-out eyes and discolouration of coral reefs.

In Kafkaf, they reported seeing yellowish oily substances and foaming where freshwater flowed into the sea, with some water emitting a smell similar to burnt tyre or cooking gas.

The report recommended further investigations into the source of the pollution and its impact on marine life.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Malaysian association donates K250,000 to five organisations
item.description: THE Malaysian Association of Papua New Guinea (MAPNG) gave a total of K250,000 to five charity organisations in Port Moresby yesterday as part of its annual donation drive. The Caritas Technical Secondary School, Gateway Children’s Fund, Canossian Sisters, Talleres De Nazaret Training Centre, and St John Ambulance Service each received K50,000.
item.content:encoded:
THE Malaysian Association of Papua New Guinea (MAPNG) gave a total of K250,000 to five charity organisations in Port Moresby yesterday as part of its annual donation drive.

The Caritas Technical Secondary School, Gateway Children’s Fund, Canossian Sisters, Talleres De Nazaret Training Centre, and St John Ambulance Service each received K50,000.

MAPNG is a prominent community charitable organisation comprising Malaysian expatriates and families living and working in PNG.

MAPNG president William Ling explained that the donation amounts were increased from K30,000 last year to K50,000 this year to help the organisations deal with inflation and the rising cost of goods and services.

The funds were raised through the annual Malaysian Business Council charity golf tournament.

In total, MAPNG will support 17 different charity organisations this year with financial aid and food supplies.

The gesture marks a milestone for the association’s leadership, as Ling announced that this would be his final year in office.

Ling said he was confident his successor would continue the good work the association had been doing in giving back to the community.

The Gateway Children’s Fund, a Christian charity based at the Wildlife settlement in the Moresby North-East electorate, plans to use the funds to support children from local settlements, including orphans and single-parent families who cannot afford school fees.

The funding will help provide school stationery, uniforms, and other essential items for students.

Monica Kaoma, the projects administrator for the Gateway Children’s Fund, expressed gratitude for the timely support.

“We really appreciate what they are doing, by giving back to the community,” Monica said.

“The donation would go directly to the kids one way or another, and we would like them to know that it is not a waste and it would make a big difference to the lives of the communities we’re serving.”

The other recipients, who are all longtime partners with MAPNG in receiving these donations shared these sentiments, stating that the vital funding would go a long way in supporting the respective services they provided across the National Capital District and Central.

The association will be making another donation worth K230,000 to four different charity organisations today. 

This includes the City Mission PNG Ltd, PNG Kidney Foundation, Operation Open Heart and the Sir Buri Kidu Heart Institution.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Road maintenance afoot to improve traffic near university
item.description: ROAD maintenance work is currently underway at Limki, between Unigate and Speedway in Lae, as authorities continue efforts to improve road conditions for motorists and nearby communities. Works project engineer Rodney Peter said that the road maintenance was funded by the National Department of Works and Highways.
item.content:encoded:
ROAD maintenance work is currently underway at Limki, between Unigate and Speedway in Lae, as authorities continue efforts to improve road conditions for motorists and nearby communities.

Works project engineer Rodney Peter said that the road maintenance was funded by the National Department of Works and Highways. 

“We are doing this because this base course layer is being laid to strengthen the road structure and provide a stable foundation for the surface,” he said.

Peter added that they were assigned to do maintenance from Bumayong market to Kamkumung roundabout as it was one of the busiest areas in Lae.

They started with excavating the road midway on Tuesday, around 9pm, and continued yesterday.

Peter said they were doing all they could to complete it.

“There are schools and a lot of business houses accessing this route so we are doing our best to smooth the problem once and for all,” he said.

The project aims to improve road access and connectivity for commuters and the surrounding communities.

While the work may cause some delays and detours in the short term, it is expected to enhance safety and ease of travel once completed.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: 10-year tax haven will stay. 10-year tax haven will stay. No debate will change terms of engagement: PNG Chiefs CEO
item.description: THERE will be no changes to the PNG Chiefs 10-year tax incentive arrangement, and that is final, according to chief executive officer Lorna McPherson. She said the agreed terms of engagement with the Chiefs have been confirmed, and no debate across Australia or elsewhere will change that.
item.content:encoded:
THERE will be no changes to the PNG Chiefs 10-year tax incentive arrangement, and that is final, according to chief executive officer Lorna McPherson.

She said the agreed terms of engagement with the Chiefs have been confirmed, and no debate across Australia or elsewhere will change that.

McPherson said the incentive package was clearly understood by all parties from the beginning and she does not expect the NRL to alter that arrangement.

“It is 10 years and there’s been no change,” the Chiefs boss said. “Everybody knew what it will be when we started this journey, so nothing has changed from our side and I don’t anticipate any changes either.”

McPherson believed recent criticism and discussion around the tax incentives only increased after the Chiefs began attracting major signings, an indication that PNG are serious about building a strong franchise.

“I think because of the signings we’ve made, people have now realised that we are here and we are serious,” she said. “It is probably a wake-up call for a few people.”

McPherson also provided an update on major infrastructure plans surrounding the Chiefs’ entry into the competition in 2028.

She confirmed discussions are continuing around expanding the Santos National Football Stadium – 15,000 currently capacity. The long-term goal is to increase capacity in stages, with the first phase expected to target between 22,000 and 25,000 seats.

The Chiefs are also looking at adding more corporate boxes to meet expected demand from sponsors and business partners.

“At the moment, there simply aren’t enough corporate boxes,” she said. “Everybody will want one, so we have to grow that area as well.”

McPherson said while final plans have not yet been confirmed, more details will be released once designs and approvals are completed.

She also confirmed progress is continuing on the club’s new Centre of Excellence, with designs currently being reviewed and signed off. Additionally, accommodation plans for the club’s future player village, involving the Constantinou Group, are also moving ahead on schedule.

McPherson praised PNG Chiefs football boss Michael Chammas for helping build momentum, hinting that more exciting player announcements are on the way.

“We’re building something special,” she said. “This is going to be an amazing journey for everybody. We want the country to get behind the team as we head towards 2028.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Nomane questions PM Marape
item.description: OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane has launched a strong attack on Prime Minister James Marape, accusing him of using rugby league and the PNG Chiefs project to distract Papua New Guineans from the country’s growing economic crisis. In a fiery press statement released on Monday, Nomane claimed the Prime Minister was more focused on rugby league publicity and international travel while families across
item.content:encoded:
OPPOSITION Leader James Nomane has launched a strong attack on Prime Minister James Marape, accusing him of using rugby league and the PNG Chiefs project to distract Papua New Guineans from the country’s growing economic crisis.

In a fiery press statement released on Monday, Nomane claimed the Prime Minister was more focused on rugby league publicity and international travel while families across the country struggled with rising fuel prices, food costs and unemployment.

The Chuave MP pointed directly to Marape’s recent public appearances around rugby league activities and reports linking Sydney Roosters utility Connor Watson to the new PNG Chiefs franchise.

Nomane said the country was facing serious challenges, including fuel shortages in Port Moresby, increasing rice prices, and the falling value of the Kina, yet the Prime Minister continued to spend time promoting rugby league projects.

The Opposition Leader described the government’s approach as “bread and circuses,” accusing Marape of entertaining the public through sport while ignoring deeper national problems.

The statement has now added more political pressure to the PNG Chiefs project, which has become one of the government’s biggest national programmes ahead of its 2028 NRL entry. Critics inside and outside politics are increasingly questioning whether the government is giving too much attention to rugby league while basic services continue to struggle.

However, government supporters argue that the PNG Chiefs project is bigger than sport alone.

The Marape government has repeatedly defended the NRL bid, saying it is part of a wider economic and national unity strategy designed to create jobs, tourism opportunities, infrastructure development, and pathways for young athletes.

Despite the criticism, Marape continues to strongly support the project, believing rugby league can help unite the country and improve PNG’s international image.

But Nomane warned that ordinary Papua New Guineans were becoming frustrated as the cost of living rose while political leaders celebrated sporting announcements.

The Opposition Leader also accused the government of failing to properly manage the economy, claiming billions of kina were now being spent on debt repayments instead of hospitals, schools, and essential services.

The latest political attack shows how rugby league is now becoming deeply connected to national politics in PNG, with the PNG Chiefs project turning into both a sporting and political battleground.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG set to take on NSW, Qld fighters in Kokoda Cup boxing show
item.description: PREPARATIONS are winding down for the eighth edition of the annual Kokoda Challenge Cup showdown between Papua New Guinea and Australia later this month in Port Moresby. Hosted by the PNG Boxing Union, the event is set for Sunday, May 31 at the Lamana Hotel Gold Club Arena in Port Moresby.
item.content:encoded:
PREPARATIONS are winding down for the eighth edition of the annual Kokoda Challenge Cup showdown between Papua New Guinea and Australia later this month in Port Moresby.

Hosted by the PNG Boxing Union, the event is set for Sunday, May 31 at the Lamana Hotel Gold Club Arena in Port Moresby.

The event is expected to attract boxing fans, corporate sponsors, sporting leaders and supporters from across the country.Union president Dr Gideon Kendino said the tournament was a celebration of national pride, discipline, unity and the growing strength of boxing in PNG as well as recognising the unique relationship between both countries.

The fight card features cadet, junior, elite male and elite female divisions.

Among the featured Queensland clashes, PNG’s Seni Roa is set to fight Sonny Prasser in the bantamweight (54kg) division, while Bid Michael Inoino takes on Jacob Friend at featherweight (55kg).

In another major contest, Neville Warupi will step into the ring against Noah McLachlan in the welterweight (67kg) elite male division.

The women’s division is also expected to draw attention with Magdalyn Menjawi set to fight Sahara Dempsey at lightweight (60kg) while Liranda Kidu will battle Katelyn Hankin in the middleweight (75kg) division.

The PNG-NSW clashes include Kua-Gunua Mua against Issac Wilton in the men’s cruiserweight (84kg) division and Warren Warupi taking on William Nolan in a middleweight (75kg) contest.

For the elite females, PNG’s top women meet NSW fighters with Goli Bali slated to fight Kiara Ortega in a flyweight (51kg) contest; Mili Saul set to take on Abbie McTeare at featherweight (57kg); Petronella Nokenoke meets Tori Beeken in their lightweight (60kg) match-up; and, Flora Loga clashes with Grace Bryant in a light middleweight (70kg) scrap.

The tournament also comes at an important time for PNG boxing as the sport continues rebuilding its profile and developing young talent around the country.

General admission tickets are selling for K100 per person, while corporate tables are available at K10,000 for a table of six.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Barras return with title
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea senior men’s cricket team, Barramundis have brought home the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup EAP Qualifier’s League Champions title after topping the standings in the Super Six stage. The team was welcomed by their families, the CPNG board chairman Michael Harrison and staff at the Jackson International terminal yesterday upon their arrival.
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea senior men’s cricket team, Barramundis have brought home the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup EAP Qualifier’s League Champions title after topping the standings in the Super Six stage.

The team was welcomed by their families, the CPNG board chairman Michael Harrison and staff at the Jackson International terminal yesterday upon their arrival.

Head coach Tim McCaskill said they are unsure what the next stage is and are waiting on an update from the ICC regarding qualification, whether that is 1x direct qualification spot or there is another 9-team qualification tournament.

“Until there is a decision made at ICC level we are unable to provide any further details of what the next stage looks like,” he said.

The players will have a short break for recovery and family time before resuming training and preparations next month – June 1.

“It’s important following tournament play to have a break because with tours of Mackay for NICC tournament and then Japan for EAP ICC T20 World Cup qualifiers, players and staff have been away from home and families for an extended period,” he said.

Meanwhile, captain Assadolla Vala praised his teammates for their efforts and confidence throughout the tournament, noting the team possessed quality players who had the opportunity to showcase their skills out there and contributed to the team’s successful run at that stage.

Vala commended debutants Aue Oru and Gaba Frank on their first international tournament as Barramundis and said the future looks bright for these two players and also for Cricket in PNG.

“We had the first opportunity of qualifying for the World Cup and at this stage we are not sure of the process going forward but if it’s a direct qualification for us then I am so proud of the boys for getting the job done in Japan,” Vala said.

“We still have a lot of polishing up to do so we will get right back to the drawing board and see what we can improve on while awaiting ICC’s update on the qualification pathway and progression to the next stage.”

He thanked Cricket PNG, the supporters and their families for all the support during their campaign.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s gold still being smuggled out
item.description: IN the days of old, much of the world traded their goods for gold as it was valued everywhere due to its rare properties. During colonial times gold was the metal the foreigners sought after and it was for gold that much of the country was discovered.
item.content:encoded:
IN the days of old, much of the world traded their goods for gold as it was valued everywhere due to its rare properties.

During colonial times gold was the metal the foreigners sought after and it was for gold that much of the country was discovered.

The “gold rush” in Morobe during the early 1900s saw the start of the country’s mining industry.

Many claim, with much justification, that gold from the Bulolo Wau gold fields built Brisbane and Sydney.

There might have been an excuse for gold smuggling in those days but today, after 50 years of sovereignty, PNG is still losing untold billions of kina in gold being smuggled out of its borders every year.

On Sept 29 last year, the Special Parliamentary Committee on Public Sector Reform and Service Delivery conducted a hearing Port Moresby to highlight the challenges affecting the country’s alluvial gold mining industry.

Most alarming was the revelation that more than two tonnes of gold had been smuggled out of the country in 2021 and 2022, costing the State millions in lost revenue.

Committee chairman and Northern Governor Gary Juffa said: “Our hearings into the Mineral Resources Authority revealed that the alluvial gold industry is in crisis due to widespread smuggling.

“Figures presented at our 2025 hearings indicate that between K1 .3 billion and K3.8 billion in gold is being smuggled out of PNG every year.

“We heard disturbing reports of illegal foreign cartels operating with impunity, destroying our environment, and breaching airport security — as evidenced by the recent interception of 28kg of gold at Jackson’s Airport after bypassing metal detectors.”

East Sepik Governor Allan Bird, also a committee member, said it was shocking to learn that there were many public service institutional gaps that had allowed for an increase in gold smuggling over the years.

“Up to 2020 with no mechanised alluvial mining, PNG had an annual export of 120,000 ounces of gold per year,” he said.

“In the last several years, foreign investment in alluvial mining has exploded.

“In fact, the last time this level of investment in alluvial mining took place in PNG was in the 1920s.

“In 2023, our alluvial gold exports dropped to 40,000 ounces (over one tonne).

“Evidence from importers overseas suggests that at least 80,000 ounces (over two tonnes) is smuggled out of our country.

“This represents a loss of US$300 million (about K1.2 billion) in foreign reserves for our country every year.

“My suspicion is that the loss is greater than this – it could be three times higher given the massive investment in mechanised mining in the sector.

“If that is indeed the case, then PNG could be losing up to US$900 million (about K3.8 billion) every year.

“We don’t know the full extent of the smuggling or the cartels that are involved in this.”

The committee’s findings do not need any more evidence as the National Airports Corporation (NAC) recently reported that six cases of attempted gold smuggling were detected at the Jackson International Airport terminal this year.

The most recent one was last Thursday, only a few weeks after another case was reported on April 27, where 20 gold bars were seized.

On Tuesday, NAC acting managing director Dominic Kaumu Kaumu called for an immediate intervention and action by all concerned organisations to safeguard Papua New Guinea’s resources.

“Illegal attempts to smuggle gold out of the country undermine national wealth and development, and economic sovereignty,” Kaumu said.

“NAC warns that any attempt to smuggle gold or other undeclared valuables will be detected, confiscated, and referred directly to PNG Customs for further action.”

The increase in gold smuggling activity has also led to PNG being grey-listed by the Financial Action Task Force (FATF).

FATF added PNG to the grey-list on Feb 6, signaling that the country has deficiencies in its framework to effectively counter money laundering an, terrorist finance and related risks.

Financial Analysis and Supervision Unit director Wilson Onea had sounded the alarm six months prior but it was a little too late.

Onea said that the ineffectiveness of government agencies responsible for ensure compliance with the law would lead to PNG put on the grey-list.

To counter this, he said: “If we can start investigating and prosecuting more, not just for gold smuggling, but for money-laundering, tax evasion, illegal logging and illegal fishing, then we can show that there is some improvement being done in the country.”

item.title: Leadership failures
item.description: THE cancellation and mismanagement of major sporting events in Papua New Guinea highlight serious failures in governance, communication, and leadership within the PNG Sports Foundation (PNGSF). Breakdowns between the board, executive management, host organising committee, and the minister’s office have resulted in poor planning, weak coordination, inadequate communication, and public embarrassment
item.content:encoded:
THE cancellation and mismanagement of major sporting events in Papua New Guinea highlight serious failures in governance, communication, and leadership within the PNG Sports Foundation (PNGSF).

Breakdowns between the board, executive management, host organising committee, and the minister’s office have resulted in poor planning, weak coordination, inadequate communication, and public embarrassment.

The PNGSF is tasked with overseeing national sports development, advising the minister, coordinating events, and safeguarding public trust.

Recent failures show the current leadership lacks the professionalism and capability to fulfil these responsibilities.

Immediate action is required: review the board, suspend or replace under-performing executives, conduct an independent inquiry, and implement reforms.

Sport in PNG is more than competition – it builds youth engagement, national pride, and social development.

PNG deserves a transparent, accountable, and competent sports administration.

Patrick Talu Wundai, Former business editor/reporter

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Funds sit idle as fuel stations run dry
item.description: THE latest fuel disruption is a serious blow to the Government, coming at the worst possible time. On paper, the K1 billion policy to keep fuel prices at April levels is sound.
item.content:encoded:
THE latest fuel disruption is a serious blow to the Government, coming at the worst possible time.

On paper, the K1 billion policy to keep fuel prices at April levels is sound.

In practice, it exposes a failure to execute the basics.

With K576 million sitting idle in a Central Bank trust account while service stations close, ordinary Papua New Guineans bear the cost.

A sum of K246 million has already reached importers, proving the system works when it moves.

The rest, however, remains stuck, causing shortages that touch transport, food, schools, hospitals, and agriculture.

Price spikes hurt the most vulnerable, undermining the policy’s intent.

Prime Minister James Marape’s threat to sack officials reveals systemic issues between Treasury, Finance, the Independent Consumer and Competition Commission, and the Central Bank.

Coordination exists on paper, but not in practice, repeating a pattern seen in medical supply delays, school subsidies, and infrastructure payments.

This is not a lack of funds or policy – it is an execution failure.

The framework and money exist; the issue is doing the work.

Until the K576 million is released and fuel flows, public trust in the Marape government and confidence in PNG’s stability remain at serious risk.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Governance risks of proposed authority
item.description: THE proposal to establish the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA), particularly ahead of the next elections requires careful scrutiny. Strengthening coordination, improving programme monitoring, and enhancing service delivery are important goals – but creating a new statutory authority may not be the most efficient or cost-effective approach.
item.content:encoded:
THE proposal to establish the National Monitoring and Coordination Authority (NMCA), particularly ahead of the next elections requires careful scrutiny.

Strengthening coordination, improving programme monitoring, and enhancing service delivery are important goals – but creating a new statutory authority may not be the most efficient or cost-effective approach.

Papua New Guinea already has a public sector framework for planning, coordination, and evaluation, including the Department of National Planning and Monitoring (DNPM), sector agencies, and provincial administrations.

The NMCA’s proposed functions largely overlap with these bodies, raising concerns about duplication rather than meaningful reform.

A new authority would bring substantial costs: leadership, staffing, office infrastructure, information and communications technology systems, and ongoing operations.

With pressing needs in health, education, infrastructure, and law and order, any additional expenditure must deliver measurable improvements.

Without strong evidence that the NMCA would outperform a strengthened DNPM, there is a risk of expanding bureaucracy without improving efficiency or service delivery.

Duplication may also weaken accountability by dispersing responsibilities and fragmenting reporting.

Experience shows that adding agencies does not automatically improve governance; poor design can create delays, inefficiencies, and confusion.

Reform should focus on strengthening existing institutions to ensure Papua New Guineans see real improvements in services, accountability, and development outcomes.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”
